title: GPU Job Scheduler — System Design
description: Design a scheduler that packs many teams' training and inference jobs onto a finite, expensive pool of GPUs. The themes are bin-packing under multi-dimensional constraints, fairness across tenants, gang scheduling for distributed jobs, and preemption.

nodes.label: Submission API
nodes.description: Accepts jobs, validates resource requests and quota, persists them.

nodes.label: Job Store
nodes.description: Authoritative job records, specs, and lifecycle state.

nodes.label: Priority Queues
nodes.description: Per-priority/per-team pending-job queues the scheduler draws from.

nodes.label: Scheduler
nodes.description: Core loop: picks jobs and places them on GPUs honoring constraints and fairness.

nodes.label: Cluster Resource Manager
nodes.description: Tracks GPU inventory, allocations, and free capacity across nodes.

nodes.label: GPU Worker Nodes
nodes.description: Machines with GPUs that run job containers under an agent.

nodes.label: Node Agent
nodes.description: Per-node daemon that launches/kills containers and reports health & usage.

nodes.label: Quota / Fairness Service
nodes.description: Enforces per-team quotas and computes fair-share priority.

nodes.label: Telemetry / Metrics
nodes.description: GPU utilization, job runtime, queue wait — drives autoscaling & fairness.

nodes.label: Preemption Controller
nodes.description: Evicts lower-priority jobs to make room for higher-priority ones.

nodes.label: Container / Image Registry
nodes.description: Stores job container images pulled by worker nodes.

nodes.label: Checkpoint Store
nodes.description: Object store for preemption/restart checkpoints; agents write here and jobs resume from checkpoint_uri.

nodes.label: Log / Event Store
nodes.description: Append-only job_events + log sink backing status and /jobs/{id}/events queries.

links.label: status events: launched / exited / lease renew

functional: Submit a job with a resource request (N GPUs, GPU type, memory, walltime) and a priority/team.
Schedule jobs onto GPU nodes honoring those constraints.
Support multi-GPU and multi-node (distributed) jobs that need all replicas to start together.
Track job lifecycle: pending -> scheduled -> running -> succeeded/failed; retry on transient failure.
nonFunctional: Maximize GPU utilization — idle GPUs are extremely expensive.
Fairness: no team starves; bursty teams can use spare capacity but yield it back.
High-priority/production jobs can preempt lower-priority/best-effort jobs.
Scheduling latency low for the queue, but throughput and packing quality matter more than per-job ms.
Resilient: a node or the scheduler crashing must not lose jobs or strand GPUs.
Multi-tenant isolation: untrusted team images share GPU nodes without leaking another team's data or escaping their quota.

capacity.label: GPUs / nodes
capacity.note: At 8 GPUs/node. Node count, not GPU count, drives heartbeat and free-list size.

capacity.label: Submit rate
capacity.note: 10K jobs/day is trivial average QPS; the API is not the bottleneck — bursts and scans are.

capacity.label: Scheduling loop
capacity.note: One scheduler loop suffices at this scale; shard by pool only when scoring can't keep the queue drained.

capacity.label: Placement work
capacity.note: Indexed free-lists by (type, mem, topology) avoid a full 1K-node scan; topology scoring is the costly part.

capacity.label: Heartbeats
capacity.note: ~25–250 hb/sec at a 5s cadence — well within one resource manager; per-GPU would be 8× that.

capacity.label: Pending queue
capacity.note: Big gangs at head-of-line can hold the queue deep; backfill keeps GPUs busy meanwhile.

capacity.label: Checkpoint I/O
capacity.note: Preemption is storage-bandwidth bound: a 200GB checkpoint at 5 GB/s ≈ 40s drain before a GPU frees.

capacity.label: Walltime range
capacity.note: Drives preemption + checkpoint design; long jobs amplify wasted work on kill-restart.

capacity.label: Cost per GPU-hour
capacity.note: Why utilization is the headline metric — every idle GPU-hour is burned money.

api.description: Submit a job with its resource request. Idempotency-Key header dedups client retries (scoped per tenant, expiring after a retention window — not globally permanent). Admission validates the resource vector against quota, the image against allowed registries + signature policy, and that the requested service account / checkpoint+log URIs belong to the caller's tenant. Returns once queued.
Get job status, placement, and attempt/checkpoint history.
Stream the job's lifecycle/log events (queued -> scheduled -> started -> checkpointed -> ...), each carrying a log-sink pointer.
Cancel a job. A queued job is marked cancelled so the scheduler skips it even if a stale queue reference remains; a running job is drained and its GPUs freed back to the resource manager.

fields.name: resources
fields.type: json resource vector (gpus, gpu_type, mig_profile, gpu_mem_gb, cpu, ram_gb, local_ssd_gb, net_tier)

fields.name: walltime
fields.type: duration (declared max runtime; bounds backfill + drives kill/requeue on overrun)

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(pending, scheduled, running, succeeded, failed, preempted, cancelled)

fields.name: placement
fields.type: list<node_id,gpu_idx> (cached projection of the live allocation — allocations is authoritative)

fields.name: checkpoint_policy
fields.type: json (enabled, interval_min, grace_target_s, resume_cmd, last_status, last_uri)

dataModel.name: gpus
dataModel.note: Resource manager's inventory (one row per physical GPU). Indexed by (gpu_type, mem_gb, node, topology_domain, health) so placement is a free-list lookup, not a full scan.

dataModel.name: quotas
dataModel.note: Per-team, per-resource-class guarantees + burst ceilings + rolling usage. One row per (team, resource_class) so fair-share can compute a dominant share across heterogeneous bottlenecks, not just GPU count.

fields.name: resource_class
fields.type: enum(gpu_a100, gpu_h100, gpu_t4, gpu_mem, mig_slice, cpu, ram) (PK part)

fields.name: guaranteed
fields.type: scarcity-normalized units (1 H100 weighs more than 1 T4; a MIG slice is fractional)

fields.name: max_burst
fields.type: units (ceiling regardless of idle capacity — anti-gaming cap)

fields.name: usage_window
fields.type: rolling unit-seconds (e.g. 24h decay) — derived from allocation intervals, not metrics

dataModel.name: allocations
dataModel.note: Authoritative allocation lifecycle — who owns which GPUs, leased and fenced. The resource manager is source of truth for freeing capacity, not metrics.

fields.name: attempt_id
fields.type: uuid (FK job_attempts — ties this allocation to one launch attempt)

fields.name: resource_vector
fields.type: json (normalized units billed for fair-share/usage accounting)

fields.name: started_at
fields.type: timestamp (allocation interval start — the billable usage clock)

fields.name: ended_at
fields.type: timestamp | null (interval end; usage_window is summed over these intervals)

dataModel.name: reservations
dataModel.note: Future-start reservation for a head-of-line gang — holds a planned start, never partial GPUs. A backfill job is admitted only if its latest-finish (now + remaining walltime) provably precedes planned_start, or it is preemptible.

fields.name: resource_shape
fields.type: json (gpus_needed, gpu_type, topology domain the gang needs)

fields.name: planned_start
fields.type: timestamp (projected from running jobs' remaining walltime)

fields.name: reason
fields.type: string (why this start: e.g. 'frees when alloc a17 hits walltime')

fields.name: expires_at
fields.type: timestamp (deadline — stale projections are recomputed, not honored forever)

dataModel.name: job_attempts
dataModel.note: One row per launch attempt — retries, preemptions, and restarts are auditable and counted.

dataModel.name: job_events
dataModel.note: Append-only lifecycle/log-pointer event stream backing status and log queries.

fields.name: kind
fields.type: enum(queued, scheduled, started, checkpointed, preempted, succeeded, failed, cancelled)

fields.name: log_sink_uri
fields.type: string (object-store prefix for this attempt's logs)

dataModel.name: nodes
dataModel.note: Node-level inventory: health, drain state, topology domain, and the agent lease the scheduler reconciles against.

patterns.name: Priority queue
patterns.what: Order pending jobs by priority and fair-share so the scheduler always considers the most deserving first.
patterns.whenToUse: Any system arbitrating scarce resources among competing requests.
patterns.group: Accept and Queue Jobs by Priority

patterns.name: Bin-packing placement
patterns.what: Fit multi-dimensional resource requests (GPUs, type, memory) onto nodes to minimize fragmentation.
patterns.whenToUse: Packing heterogeneous jobs onto finite heterogeneous hardware.

patterns.name: Gang scheduling
patterns.what: Allocate all GPUs of a distributed job at once or none — avoid partial holds that deadlock.
patterns.whenToUse: Distributed training where every replica must start together.

patterns.name: Preemption
patterns.what: Evict lower-priority jobs (after checkpoint) to free room for higher-priority work.
patterns.whenToUse: When production work must beat best-effort work for capacity.

patterns.name: Dominant-resource fairness
patterns.what: Allocate by each team's most-constrained resource share so no tenant starves.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-tenant clusters with quotas and bursting.

patterns.name: Reconciliation loop
patterns.what: Continuously drive actual cluster state toward desired job state; recover by re-reconciling.
patterns.whenToUse: Control planes that must self-heal after crashes.

patterns.name: Tenant admission & isolation
patterns.what: Validate images, identities, and resource/log ACLs at submit so untrusted team workloads can share GPU nodes safely.
patterns.whenToUse: Any multi-tenant platform running arbitrary user code on shared infrastructure.
patterns.group: Accept and Queue Jobs by Priority

tradeoff: Kubernetes plus Volcano or Kueue fits cloud-native teams that want gang scheduling, quotas, and device-plugin integration while still owning scheduler policy.
Slurm is strong for HPC backfill, reservations, and fair-share, but multi-tenant container admission and cloud IAM integration take extra work.
Ray is effective for Python ML workloads and elastic jobs, but it is weaker as the single authority for heterogeneous multi-tenant cluster scheduling.
Managed training platforms reduce scheduler operations, but they constrain custom packing, preemption, topology, and fairness policy.
makesIrrelevant: A fully managed training platform can absorb queueing, retries, and checkpointing, removing much of the bespoke control plane at the cost of policy control.

tradeoff: Relational stores are the default for the job state machine, attempts, allocations, reservations, cancellations, and fair-share queries.
etcd works well for Kubernetes-native desired state and leases, but large history and audit tables belong in a database.
Strongly consistent distributed stores help at very large cluster or multi-region scale, but add operational and schema discipline.
makesIrrelevant: A transactional job/allocation store makes an external durable queue optional because pending work can be rebuilt from indexed pending rows.

tradeoff: Keeping the queue as a DB index (default) avoids a second system and keeps the store authoritative.
A dedicated broker lowers dispatch latency and gives broker-native DLQs, but reintroduces DB/queue reconciliation for cancellation, reprioritization, and recovery.
At roughly 0.12 average submits/sec, queue correctness and recoverability matter more than broker throughput.
makesIrrelevant: If the store is authoritative and submit rate is low, a separate durable broker is often unnecessary.

technologyChoices.concern: GPU inventory, device plugins, and capacity pools
tradeoff: Kubernetes device plugins and GPU Operator integrate cleanly with pods, MIG, drivers, and taints, but scheduler decisions still need topology and fragmentation awareness.
Slurm GRES models GPU devices and partitions well for HPC clusters, but integration with container registries and cloud identities is less native.
A custom resource-manager index gives precise free-list and topology control, but it must be reconciled against node agents and health events.
makesIrrelevant: A managed GPU node-pool service removes much of the driver bootstrap work, but not the scheduler's need to track leases, health, topology, and fragmentation.

tradeoff: A memory-resident free-list index gives fast 10-100 candidate scoring, but must be rebuilt from durable allocations after scheduler restart.
A database-backed inventory is simpler and auditable, but topology-aware scoring can become query-heavy.
General solvers can improve packing quality for small batches, but a deterministic heuristic is easier to bound inside the scheduling loop.
makesIrrelevant: If the cluster is small and homogeneous, a simple indexed best-fit heuristic can avoid a separate optimizer or solver.

technologyChoices.concern: Fair-share quota and usage accounting ledger
tradeoff: The authoritative fair-share ledger should be derived from allocation intervals and attempts, not raw metrics.
A relational ledger is enough for live scheduling decisions; analytics stores help with long rolling windows, audits, and chargeback.
Metrics systems are useful for utilization signals and SLOs, but they should not free GPUs or set billable usage.
makesIrrelevant: A durable allocation ledger removes the need to trust sampled GPU metrics for quota accounting.

tradeoff: Object storage is cheap, durable, and tenant-ACL-friendly for checkpoints and logs, but bandwidth caps how fast preemption can drain a GPU.
Parallel filesystems improve checkpoint throughput for large models, but cost more and need stricter placement near the compute pool.
Local scratch helps runtime performance but cannot be the only checkpoint target because node loss must not lose restart state.
makesIrrelevant: A managed object store with IAM, lifecycle policies, and encryption removes most bespoke checkpoint/log retention work.

technologyChoices.concern: Node agent, container runtime, and launch control
tradeoff: Kubernetes-native agents reuse kubelet lifecycle, CRI, service accounts, and runtime classes, but custom launch tokens and allocation leases still need integration.
A standalone systemd agent gives full launch and checkpoint control for non-Kubernetes clusters, but must implement health, logging, identity, and upgrade behavior.
The runtime must expose idempotent launch acknowledgements and graceful termination hooks so preemption and retry do not double-run jobs.
makesIrrelevant: If kubelet is the authoritative node agent, a separate per-node launcher may be unnecessary; the scheduler can express desired pods/jobs and reconcile status.

tradeoff: Policy-as-code admission (OPA/Kyverno) is flexible and auditable but is one more control-plane component to operate and keep correct.
Sandboxed runtimes (gVisor/Kata) harden multi-tenant nodes but add per-container overhead — often reserved for untrusted/best-effort tenants.
Per-team object-store ACLs + workload identity remove inline secrets but require the registry, IdP, and storage to agree on tenant identity.
makesIrrelevant: Strict single-tenant-per-node placement (exclusive nodes) sidesteps most runtime-isolation concerns at the cost of utilization.

technologyChoices.concern: Lifecycle events, status queries, and log streaming
tradeoff: An append-only job_events table is simple and keeps lifecycle transitions queryable with job state.
A stream/log pipeline is better for fanout, live tailing, and retention tiers, but the job state machine must remain authoritative.
Object-store log sinks scale cheaply, while indexed log systems are needed for fast operator search and incident response.
makesIrrelevant: A durable event stream with log pointers removes ad hoc polling tables for status, logs, and retry audit.

tradeoff: Kubernetes Lease objects (etcd-backed) are the simplest path if the control plane already runs on K8s.
Standalone etcd/ZooKeeper/Consul give leader election plus the fencing epoch for any deployment, at the cost of running a quorum service.
Database-backed locks are acceptable for a single scheduler loop when the lock write and epoch fencing share the same durable store.
Metrics should track queue wait by team and priority, placement success, fragmentation, lease expiry, checkpoint duration, and preemption outcomes.
Tracing is useful for submit-to-scheduled latency and agent launch paths, but high-cardinality job IDs must be sampled or bounded.
Managed observability reduces storage and dashboard operations, but export format and retention policy can limit deep scheduler debugging.
makesIrrelevant: A managed metrics/logs/tracing stack removes much of the bespoke SLO dashboard plumbing, but not the need to define scheduler-specific signals.

technologyChoices.concern: GPU node-pool provisioning and autoscaling hooks
tradeoff: Autoscaling GPU nodes can reduce idle spend, but cold-start time, image pulls, and capacity scarcity mean the scheduler still needs backfill and fair-share.
Karpenter-style provisioning works well for heterogeneous node shapes when pending jobs express exact resource vectors.
Static pools are simpler and predictable for scarce reserved GPUs, but waste capacity when demand shifts across teams or GPU types.
makesIrrelevant: For a fixed on-prem GPU cluster, autoscaling machinery is mostly irrelevant; the same signals still help decide capacity planning and maintenance drains.

steps.title: 1. Naive: FIFO Queue onto the Next Free GPU (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. Jobs go into one first-in-first-out queue; the scheduler takes the head job and assigns it to the next free GPU, one job per GPU.

This is genuinely fine for a tiny single-team lab cluster running single-GPU jobs — worth saying, because it frames the rest of the complexity as justified only at shared-cluster scale. But at that scale it exposes the problems the rest of the design exists to solve. There is no notion of priority or fairness, so one team's flood of jobs starves everyone behind it and a production job waits behind hours of best-effort work. A distributed job needing 64 GPUs at once can't be expressed — FIFO would hand it GPUs one at a time, and two such jobs deadlock holding half the cluster each. And matching a job only to 'a free GPU' ignores GPU type and memory, so jobs land on hardware that can't run them and capacity fragments.
So the baseline motivates everything that follows: priority/fair-share queueing, constrained bin-packing, gang scheduling, and preemption.

view.caption: An engineer submits a job to the API, which enqueues it on the priority queues; the scheduler dequeues a candidate and asks the resource manager for fitting GPUs across worker nodes.

steps.decisionPrompt: A single FIFO queue hands each job the next free GPU. What happens when a production job sits behind a best-effort flood, when a 64-GPU training job arrives, and when a job needs a specific GPU type?
whyNow: Establishing the FIFO baseline first makes it obvious why the scheduler needs priority/fairness, multi-dimensional bin-packing, all-or-nothing gang placement, and preemption — each later step removes a specific failure from here.

recap.after: Jobs run one-per-GPU in submission order.
recap.newRisk: No priority or fairness (teams starve each other), no way to co-schedule a distributed job (partial holds deadlock), and no constraint matching (jobs land on wrong/too-small GPUs and capacity fragments).

traps.trap: Shipping FIFO 'next free GPU' as 'good enough'.
traps.why: It ignores priority and fairness, can't gang-schedule multi-GPU jobs without deadlock, and wastes/fragments GPUs by ignoring type and memory constraints.
traps.instead: Order by priority + fair-share, bin-pack under constraints, gang-schedule distributed jobs all-or-nothing, and preempt lower-priority work.

steps.title: 2. Accept and Queue Jobs by Priority
description: The submission API validates the resource request against the team's quota, persists the job as pending in the job store, and enqueues it into a priority queue.
Submission is idempotent: the client supplies an Idempotency-Key, stored as a unique idempotency_key on jobs, so a retried POST returns the existing job rather than creating a duplicate.
Separate queues (or one priority-ordered queue) per priority class — prod, batch, best-effort — so the scheduler always pulls the most important runnable job.

Admission is also where multi-tenant security is enforced, because the cluster runs arbitrary team images on shared GPU nodes. The API validates that the image comes from an allowed registry and is signed (with a digest, not a mutable tag), rejects privileged/host-mount requests, and pins a runtime class (e.g. a sandboxed runtime). It binds the job to the caller's namespace/project and service account, and checks that the requested checkpoint and log-sink URIs are owned by that tenant — a team can't write to or read another team's checkpoints/logs. Secrets are mounted via the tenant's identity, never passed inline.
The Idempotency-Key is scoped per tenant/client and expires after a retention window, so keys don't collide across teams and the dedup table doesn't grow forever.

Persisting before enqueueing means a crash never loses an accepted job; the queue is rebuildable from pending jobs in the store. Cancellation flips the durable state to cancelled, so the scheduler skips it even if a stale queue reference lingers.

view.caption: The submission API checks the quota service, persists the job to the job store, and enqueues it on the priority queues by priority.

options.name: Database is source of truth, queue is a fast index (default)
options.description: The submission API persists each accepted job as pending in the job store first, then enqueues a lightweight priority-ordered reference the scheduler dequeues.
pros: A submitted job is durable the moment it's persisted, so a queue or scheduler crash never loses pending work.
The queue carries only lightweight references ordered by priority, keeping enqueue/dequeue cheap.
Quota and audit history live alongside job state in one queryable store.
cons: Every dispatch must reconcile queue position against DB state, adding a read.
Two systems to keep consistent — a job can be in the queue but failed in the DB.
Queue rebuild after a restart requires scanning the DB for pending jobs.

view.caption: The job store is the source of truth that the API persists to, while the priority queue is a fast index the scheduler dequeues from.

options.name: Queue is the source of truth
options.description: The accepted job lives only in the priority queue, and the scheduler dequeues directly from it as the authoritative next job with no separate store.
pros: One system, no DB/queue reconciliation — dequeue is the authoritative next job.
Lowest dispatch latency: the scheduler reads straight from the queue.
Simplest possible pipeline for a small cluster.
cons: A broker crash or eviction loses every pending job unless the queue is itself durable and replicated.
Hard to query history, fairness usage, or 'all jobs for team X' — queues aren't relational.
Re-prioritizing or cancelling a queued job means surgery on queue internals.

view.caption: The API enqueues the job straight onto the priority queues as the source of truth, and the scheduler dequeues candidates directly from there.

steps.decisionPrompt: Should the queue be the source of truth for pending jobs, or the database?
whyNow: Submission/queueing is the entry point; making the store authoritative here is what lets the scheduler crash and recover later.

flows.name: Idempotent submit + cancel
flows.note: A retried POST with the same Idempotency-Key returns the existing job. Cancellation flips durable state so the scheduler skips a stale queue reference.

messages.label: dequeue -> sees cancelled in store -> skip

recap.after: Jobs durably accepted (idempotently) and ordered by priority.
recap.newRisk: Queue ordering alone doesn't place jobs — need a scheduler that respects hardware constraints.

traps.trap: Treating the in-memory queue as the only record of pending jobs.
traps.why: A scheduler restart would lose every queued job.
traps.instead: Persist jobs first; rebuild the queue from the store on startup.

traps.trap: Accepting any image, command, and checkpoint/log URI a tenant supplies.
traps.why: On shared GPU nodes a team could run a privileged container, pull an unsigned image, or point its log/checkpoint URI at another team's bucket and exfiltrate their data.
traps.instead: Enforce admission: signed images from allowed registries (by digest), no privileged/host mounts, a sandboxed runtime class, and tenant-scoped service accounts + checkpoint/log ACLs.

steps.title: 3. Place Jobs: Bin-Packing onto GPUs
description: The scheduler loop pulls the top candidate and asks the resource manager for a set of free GPUs matching the full request vector (count, GPU type/profile, GPU memory, and any CPU/RAM/local-storage the sidecars need). The resource manager keeps indexed free-lists — by GPU type/MIG profile, memory, node, topology domain, health, drain state, and runtime/driver compatibility — so this is a lookup over ~10–100 candidates, not a full scan of every node on each decision.
This is multi-dimensional bin-packing: pack jobs to minimize fragmentation so large jobs can still find contiguous capacity later. Prefer filling partially-used nodes (best-fit) over scattering.

Isolation also constrains placement: whether a node may co-locate jobs from different tenants is a policy choice. Sensitive workloads get exclusive GPUs or whole-node placement; MIG slices give hardware-isolated partitions for small jobs; taints/drains keep jobs off nodes under maintenance or with incompatible drivers.
On placement, mark the GPUs allocated, update the job to scheduled, and hand it to the node agent to launch the container.

view.caption: The scheduler dequeues a candidate, asks the resource manager for fitting GPUs, updates the job store, and places it on a node agent that pulls the image and launches the container.

options.name: Best-fit bin-packing (consolidate) (default)
options.description: The scheduler packs each job onto the fewest, fullest matching nodes, co-locating its GPUs and leaving whole nodes free for future multi-GPU jobs.
pros: Packs a job onto the fewest, fullest nodes, leaving large contiguous gaps for future multi-GPU jobs.
Co-locates a job's GPUs on one node, so NVLink/local interconnect speeds intra-job traffic.
Keeps whole nodes free, enabling power-down and reducing fragmentation for gang jobs.
cons: Hot nodes run near capacity, so a node failure evicts more jobs at once.
Thermal and bandwidth contention rises when GPUs are densely packed.
Greedy packing can still strand small fragments across already-busy nodes.

view.caption: Best-fit placement: the resource manager picks the most-packed node with room so the agent launches the container there, consolidating jobs onto fewer nodes.

options.name: Worst-fit spread (load balance)
options.description: The scheduler places each job on the most-empty matching nodes, spreading load so any single node failure takes down fewer jobs.
pros: Spreads jobs across the most-empty nodes, so any single node failure takes fewer jobs down.
Lower per-node thermal and PCIe/network contention improves throughput for bandwidth-bound jobs.
Simple to reason about and avoids hotspots under bursty submission.
cons: Fragments free GPUs across many nodes, starving large gang jobs of contiguous capacity.
A multi-GPU job's workers land on different nodes, so cross-node interconnect bottlenecks it.
No nodes go fully idle, blocking power-down savings.

view.caption: Worst-fit placement: the scheduler asks the resource manager for the emptiest fitting node so the agent spreads jobs across worker nodes.

options.name: Topology-aware scoring
options.description: The scheduler scores candidate placements by interconnect topology (NVLink domain, rack, zone) and picks the highest-scoring fit for the job's communication pattern.
pros: Scores candidate placements by interconnect topology (same NVLink domain, same rack, same zone) for the job's communication pattern.
Gives the best end-to-end throughput for tightly-coupled distributed training.
Can blend pack vs spread per job class instead of one global policy.
cons: Requires accurate, fresh topology metadata from the resource manager — more state to maintain.
Scoring is more CPU-expensive per scheduling decision, slowing dispatch under load.
Over-constraining on topology can leave jobs pending when an ideal fit doesn't exist.

view.caption: The resource manager scores candidates against a topology map of the interconnect graph so the scheduler places tightly-coupled jobs on well-connected GPUs.

steps.decisionPrompt: A job needs 4 A100s. You have 3 free on one node and 5 scattered across others. What do you do?

concepts.definition: Fitting items of varying sizes into bins to minimize wasted space / bin count.
concepts.whyItMatters: Poor packing fragments GPUs so large jobs can't fit even when total free GPUs suffice.
concepts.example: Best-fit onto partially-used nodes keeps whole nodes free for big gang jobs.

whyNow: Placement is the core scheduling decision; framing it as constrained bin-packing shows you understand fragmentation, the central efficiency problem.

flows.name: Best-fit placement and launch
flows.note: The scheduler asks the resource manager for a best-fit set of free GPUs, reserves the allocation, marks the job scheduled, and hands it to the node agent to launch.

recap.after: Jobs packed onto matching GPUs and launched.
recap.newRisk: Multi-GPU distributed jobs can grab some GPUs and wait for the rest — a deadlock; need gang scheduling.

steps.title: 4. Gang Scheduling for Distributed Jobs
description: A distributed training job needs all N GPUs simultaneously. If the scheduler grants GPUs incrementally, two big jobs can each hold half the cluster and deadlock waiting for the rest.

Gang scheduling allocates all-or-nothing: only place the job when every GPU it needs is available at once; otherwise leave it pending and hold no GPUs. A partial gang never starts and never sits on idle GPUs.

To stop a large gang from starving behind a stream of small jobs, give it a future-start reservation: a planned start time and priority position, not held GPUs. The planned_start is projected from the remaining walltime of the running jobs whose GPUs the gang will inherit, the reservation records why (which allocations free it) and an expiry so a stale projection is recomputed rather than honored forever.

Backfill is admitted by an explicit predicate, not by hope: a candidate runs in the gap only if its latest-finish — now plus its remaining declared walltime — provably precedes the reservation's planned_start, or if it is preemptible (so the scheduler can reclaim it at planned_start). If a backfilled job overruns its declared walltime, the scheduler enforces it: checkpoint-and-requeue (or kill) at the walltime so it can't push the gang's start. Without this enforcement, the 'reservation' is only advisory and large gangs can still starve.

view.caption: For a multi-GPU job the scheduler dequeues the gang, asks the resource manager for all fitting GPUs at once, and places it via the agent only when the whole set is available.

options.name: All-or-nothing reservation (default)
options.description: The scheduler places a distributed job only when every GPU it needs is free at once, acquiring the whole set atomically or leaving the job pending holding nothing.
pros: A 64-GPU job acquires all 64 atomically or none, so no job holds partial resources waiting for the rest.
Eliminates the deadlock where two big jobs each grab 40 and block forever.
Workers start together, which is required for synchronized distributed training.
cons: A huge job can wait a long time for enough simultaneous free GPUs (head-of-line blocking).
Reserving and releasing the whole set atomically needs careful coordination in the scheduler.
A brief utilization dip can occur at planned_start if no backfill job fit the remaining gap — but no GPUs are ever held idle by the pending gang itself.

view.caption: All-or-nothing: the scheduler acquires the full set of GPUs from the resource manager before placing any task, so a partial gang never starts.

options.name: Partial reservation with timeout (rejected)
options.description: GPUs are granted to a large gang as they free up and held in a partial reservation, with a timeout releasing the hold if the full set never assembles. Rejected: held GPUs are wasted compute and two big gangs can still deadlock.
pros: GPUs are grabbed the moment they free up, so the gang assembles without waiting for one simultaneous window.
A hold timeout eventually releases a stuck partial reservation, avoiding permanent deadlock.
Conceptually simple to start — just keep acquiring until you have enough.
cons: A distributed job can't start until every worker is up, so partially-held GPUs are pure wasted compute the whole time.
Two big gangs can each grab half the cluster and live-lock acquiring and releasing each other's GPUs.
Timeout tuning is delicate — too short thrashes, too long wastes capacity. This is the failure mode all-or-nothing exists to avoid.

view.caption: Rejected: the scheduler incrementally holds GPUs in a partial reservation with a timeout — those GPUs idle until the gang completes, and two big gangs can deadlock.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two 64-GPU jobs each acquire 40 GPUs and wait for 24 more. What went wrong and how do you prevent it?

concepts.definition: Co-scheduling all tasks of a job at once, or none.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents partial allocations that waste GPUs and deadlock distributed jobs.
concepts.example: A 64-GPU job stays pending until 64 are free together; it never holds 40 idle.

concepts.definition: Running short jobs around a large job's future-start reservation, admitted only if their latest-finish (now + remaining walltime) provably precedes the reservation's planned start, or they are preemptible.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps utilization high without delaying the reserved gang — and crucially never runs on GPUs held by a partial gang (there are none). The walltime predicate is what makes the reservation enforceable rather than advisory.
concepts.example: The 64-GPU gang is reserved to start at T+10min; a 5-min 1-GPU job backfills the gap because 5min < 10min; a 30-min job is rejected unless it's preemptible.

whyNow: Gang scheduling is the GPU-specific twist interviewers probe; naming the partial-allocation deadlock and backfill is the differentiator.

recap.before: Incremental allocation could deadlock big jobs.
recap.after: Distributed jobs start atomically; a future-start reservation protects head-of-line gangs while short backfill jobs keep GPUs busy.
recap.newRisk: Large gangs may still wait long under load, and best-effort work must yield to production — enter preemption/fairness.

traps.trap: Allocating GPUs to a multi-node job as they free up, or backfilling onto a gang's partially-held GPUs.
traps.why: Two big jobs can hold half the cluster each and neither can start — deadlock with idle GPUs. And a partial gang's held GPUs are idle compute, not free room to backfill.
traps.instead: All-or-nothing gang placement; give the gang a future-start reservation (no GPUs held) and backfill only short jobs that finish before that start.

flows.name: Gang scheduling: all-or-nothing placement
flows.note: A distributed job is placed only when every GPU it needs is free at once; otherwise it stays pending holding nothing. It gets a future-start reservation, and only short backfill jobs that finish before that planned start run in the meantime — never on the gang's reserved capacity.

messages.label: no -> hold no GPUs, stay pending
reserve future start for gang (no GPUs held)
backfill: run only short jobs that finish before planned start

steps.title: 5. Fairness and Quotas Across Teams
description: Each team has a guaranteed share and a burst ceiling. A team may use idle capacity beyond its guarantee, but must yield it when others need their guaranteed share.

Compute scheduling priority from fair-share. A concrete sketch: score = priorityClass + teamWeight × (guaranteed − rollingUsage) / guaranteed + ageBoost. A team far under its share (or with a starving, aged job) floats up; a team over its guarantee on burst capacity sinks. rollingUsage is normalized unit-seconds over a decaying window (quotas.usage_window), so a team that just finished a big run is briefly deprioritized.

Dominant-resource fairness generalizes this when jobs differ in their bottleneck resource: fairness is computed on each team's dominant share — the max over resource classes (scarcity-normalized GPU-count by type, GPU-memory, MIG slices, CPU/RAM) — not on a single GPU count. The quotas table holds one row per (team, resource_class), and a job's resources vector says which classes it consumes; one scarce H100 weighs more than one T4.

Usage is accounted from durable state, not raw metrics. Billable usage is summed from allocation intervals (allocations.started_at/ended_at × resource_vector) and attempt records — these can't lag, drop samples, or be spoofed by a broken agent. Metrics enriches utilization and health decisions (a job pinning low GPU util is a candidate to investigate), but never frees a GPU or sets fair-share usage. The quotas.usage_window is therefore derived from the allocation ledger, with metrics as an advisory overlay.
Guard against gaming: bill against requested walltime/size (not just used), reclaim overruns, and cap how much any team can borrow (max_burst) regardless of idle capacity.

view.caption: The scheduler asks the quota/fairness service for fair-share priority before dequeuing; the service derives billable usage from durable allocation intervals in the job store, with metrics only an advisory utilization signal.

options.name: Hierarchical fair-share with borrowing (default)
options.description: Scheduling priority is computed from each team's rolling usage against its guarantee, letting teams burst onto idle capacity and reclaiming it via preemption when the owner returns.
pros: Team B can burst onto Team A's idle GPUs, maximizing cluster utilization.
When Team A submits, fair-share reclaims its guaranteed share (via preemption of borrowed capacity).
Dominant-resource fairness across per-resource-class quotas handles mixed GPU-type/memory/CPU demands, not just GPU count.
cons: Reclaim requires preempting Team B's borrowed jobs, which only pays off if those jobs checkpoint.
Computing fair shares from the allocation ledger adds a feedback loop (cheaper and harder to spoof than a metrics loop, but still state to maintain).
'Fair' is contested — teams argue over weights and guarantees.

view.caption: Hierarchical fair-share lets the quota service raise a team's priority by borrowing idle capacity, computed from durable allocation intervals with metrics as an advisory signal.

options.name: Hard per-team quotas (no borrowing)
options.description: Each team gets a fixed slice of the cluster it can never exceed, so jobs only run within their own quota and no reclaim or preemption is ever needed.
pros: Dead simple and predictable: a team can never exceed its allotment, so no reclaim or preemption is needed.
No noisy-neighbor effects — Team A's capacity is always instantly available to it.
Easy to bill and reason about; each team owns a fixed slice.
cons: Idle GPUs in one team's quota sit unused while another team's jobs queue — poor utilization.
No way to absorb bursts, so expensive GPUs are wasted during off-peak hours.
Encourages teams to over-request quota 'just in case', worsening waste.

view.caption: The quota service enforces hard per-team caps with no borrowing, so the scheduler only dequeues jobs within a team's fixed share.

steps.decisionPrompt: Team A is idle and Team B is bursting on A's unused GPUs. Then A submits a big job. What happens?

concepts.definition: Prioritize jobs to drive each tenant toward its fair allocation over time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents one busy team from monopolizing a shared cluster.
concepts.example: A team under its guarantee is scheduled ahead of one over it.

concepts.term: Worked example: gang + backfill + fair-share together
concepts.definition: One scenario tying the mechanisms together on a 128-GPU cluster, 120 busy.
concepts.whyItMatters: Shows how reservation, backfill admission, and a fair-share shift interact in a single timeline.
concepts.example: Team Vision submits a 64-GPU gang; only 8 are free, so it gets a future-start reservation at T+12min (when a running 56-GPU job hits its walltime). The scheduler backfills the 8 free GPUs with two 1-GPU jobs whose walltimes (4min, 9min) both clear T+12min; a 20-min job is rejected. Then Team NLP, far under its guarantee, returns and submits prod work: fair-share floats it above Vision's borrowed best-effort jobs, the preemption controller checkpoints-and-requeues two of them, and NLP runs — but the 64-GPU reservation is untouched because no GPU was ever held by the pending gang.

whyNow: Multi-tenancy is what makes this a hard scheduling problem rather than a single queue; fairness is the requirement most candidates underweight.

recap.after: Priority blends class + fair-share; bursting is reclaimable.
recap.newRisk: Reclaiming bursted capacity means evicting running jobs — preemption.

steps.title: 6. Preemption with Checkpointing
description: When a high-priority job has no room, the preemption controller selects victims among lower-priority/best-effort jobs and evicts them to free GPUs. Only jobs that advertised preemptible:true are eligible.

Evict gracefully: send SIGTERM, the agent checkpoints to the checkpoint store (writing checkpoint_uri back), and a bounded grace window elapses before SIGKILL. Because checkpoint I/O is storage-bandwidth bound (a 200GB checkpoint can take tens of seconds), the grace window and concurrent-preemption count must be bounded so eviction doesn't stall the incoming job.
Choose victims to minimize wasted work: lowest priority first, then least progress / cheapest to redo, then best fit for the incoming job's shape (so one eviction frees exactly the GPUs needed).

Throttle thrashing: each job has a max_preemptions cap (after which it becomes non-preemptible for a cooldown), and a recently-preempted job gets a brief anti-thrash grace before it can be evicted again.

Persist the checkpoint policy, not just the last URI: jobs.checkpoint_policy stores whether checkpointing is enabled, the interval, the grace target, the resume command, and the last checkpoint status, so any scheduler instance can decide eligibility and resume correctly after a restart — not only the one that placed the job.

view.caption: When the scheduler needs room it calls the preemption controller, which tells the agent to checkpoint and evict a victim; the agent writes to the checkpoint store and the scheduler requeues the job.

options.name: Checkpoint-and-requeue (default)
options.description: The controller signals victim jobs to checkpoint and gives them a grace period, then kills and requeues them so they resume from the last checkpoint when capacity returns.
pros: Signals the victim to checkpoint, so 6 hours of training survive and the job resumes from the last checkpoint.
Lets a high-priority prod job get GPUs quickly without permanently destroying low-priority work.
Preempted job goes back on the queue and runs again when capacity frees up.
cons: Needs a graceful drain window and jobs that actually implement checkpointing.
Checkpoint I/O is expensive for large model state and adds latency before the GPU frees.
A malicious or buggy job that ignores the checkpoint signal must still be force-killed.

view.caption: Checkpoint-and-requeue: the preemption controller has the agent checkpoint to the checkpoint store and evict the victim, and the scheduler requeues it in the job store to resume later.

options.name: Kill-and-restart (no checkpoint)
options.description: The controller terminates victim containers immediately with no drain window and requeues them to rerun from scratch.
pros: Frees the GPU almost instantly — no drain window, just terminate the container.
Trivial to implement; no checkpoint plumbing or storage required.
Fine for short or idempotent best-effort jobs that are cheap to rerun.
cons: Throws away all in-progress work, so preempting a 6-hour job wastes 6 GPU-hours.
Restarted job redoes everything from scratch, amplifying total cluster waste under churn.
Frequent preemption of long jobs makes them effectively unschedulable.

view.caption: Kill-and-restart: the preemption controller has the agent kill the victim outright and the scheduler marks it for a fresh restart in the job store.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you preempt a 6-hour training job for a prod job without throwing away 6 hours of work?

concepts.definition: Save a preempted job's state, kill it, and resume from the checkpoint later.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes preemption cheap enough to use for fairness/priority without massive waste.
concepts.example: Best-effort job checkpoints to storage, is requeued, resumes when GPUs free up.

whyNow: Preemption is how priority and fairness are actually enforced under contention; the checkpointing nuance separates strong answers.

flows.name: Graceful preemption: checkpoint and requeue
flows.note: To free room for a prod job, the controller SIGTERMs a victim, the agent checkpoints within the grace window (SIGKILL on timeout), and the freed GPUs go back while the victim is requeued to resume from its checkpoint.

recap.before: High-priority jobs waited behind long best-effort jobs.
recap.after: Priority is enforced; victims resume from checkpoint.
recap.newRisk: Thrashing if preemption is too aggressive; node/scheduler crashes must not strand GPUs.

traps.why: Throws away hours of training and wastes the GPUs already spent.

steps.title: 7. Reliability: Reconciliation and Failure Recovery
description: Capacity is freed by authoritative agent status events to the resource manager (job exited / node lost) plus durable attempt records — not by a metrics signal. Metrics observes utilization and health; it must never be the source of truth for freeing a GPU, or a slow/partitioned node looks idle and its job gets double-run.

The scheduler is a reconciliation loop: desired state (jobs that should run) is in the durable store, actual state is reported by agents. On restart it rebuilds from the store and re-reconciles — no scheduling state lives only in memory.

Allocations are leased and fenced. Each allocation carries the scheduler_epoch that made it and a lease the agent must renew; a launch_token makes the launch idempotent so a retried placement can't double-run. A single active scheduler is chosen by leader election, and its epoch fences a stale or partitioned former leader — its writes are rejected as old.
Distinguish a dead node from a slow one (heartbeat timeout + failure detector) before rescheduling, and resume from the last checkpoint where available.

The lifecycle is a small state machine the agent and scheduler drive idempotently: pending → scheduled → running → {succeeded | failed | preempted | cancelled}, where failed/preempted go back to pending (retry/resume) until the retry policy or max_preemptions is exhausted. Each transition is one job_event and (for running) one allocation interval. An allocation belongs to exactly one job_attempt (attempt_id), so a duplicate launch token or a stale-epoch write is rejected rather than creating a second live attempt. jobs.placement and gpus.allocated_to are cached projections of the authoritative allocations rows; on reconcile the scheduler rebuilds them from allocations and never trusts a divergent cache.

Operational SLOs make reconciliation testable: scheduling p95 (queue-to-placed) under a target, max reconcile lag (how stale actual-vs-desired may be) bounded, lease renewal cadence shorter than lease TTL, node-failure detection within a few missed heartbeats, and a checkpoint-drain bound so preemption can't stall indefinitely. The control plane exports counters for these: stale-lease reclaims, duplicate launch-token rejections, queue wait by team/priority, fragmentation, checkpoint duration, and preemption success/failure.

view.caption: Agents send authoritative status events to the resource manager, write attempt records to the job store, and append lifecycle/log events to the event store; the resource manager frees capacity from those events while the scheduler reconciles desired state under a fenced lease.

steps.decisionPrompt: The scheduler process crashes while 500 jobs are running. What state survives, and how does it recover?

concepts.definition: Repeatedly compare desired vs actual state and act to close the gap.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes the control plane self-healing and crash-tolerant.
concepts.example: On restart, the scheduler reads running/pending jobs from the store and resumes placement.

concepts.definition: An allocation carries a monotonic scheduler_epoch and a time-bounded lease the owner must renew; a higher epoch fences out a stale writer.
concepts.whyItMatters: Stops a partitioned old scheduler or a duplicate launch from double-acting on the same GPUs.
concepts.example: Resource manager rejects a free/launch stamped with epoch 7 once epoch 8 has been elected.

concepts.definition: Valid transitions pending → scheduled → running → {succeeded | failed | preempted | cancelled}, with failed/preempted returning to pending until retry/preemption caps are hit.
concepts.whyItMatters: Retries, cancellation, preemption, lease expiry, node loss, and scheduler restart all mutate overlapping state; only valid transitions plus idempotent attempt records keep it reconcilable.
concepts.example: A preempted attempt closes its allocation interval and emits a 'preempted' event; the job returns to pending and resumes from checkpoint_uri on the next attempt.

whyNow: Tying it back to durable state + reconciliation shows the whole control plane survives crashes — the reliability requirement.

flows.name: Scheduler restart recovery
flows.note: A new leader is elected with a fresh epoch, loads desired state from the store, reconciles actual allocations against live agent status, and fences any stale-epoch writes. Running jobs are untouched.

messages.label: win election; load desired state (epoch N+1)
fence epoch <= N; requeue jobs with expired leases

recap.after: Crash-tolerant control plane that re-derives state from the store.
recap.newRisk: Reconciliation lag; split-brain if a partitioned scheduler keeps acting (needs leader election).

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Heartbeat timeout marks it down; its jobs are requeued (from checkpoint where available) and its GPUs removed from inventory.
failureDrills.mitigation: Failure detector + reschedule from durable job state.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It rebuilds queues and allocations from the store and resumes; running jobs are untouched.
failureDrills.mitigation: Durable job/allocation state + reconciliation; leader election for a single active scheduler.

failureDrills.scenario: Split-brain: a partitioned former leader keeps issuing placements while a new leader is elected.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The resource manager rejects the old leader's writes because they carry a stale scheduler_epoch; only the new leader's epoch is authoritative, so no GPU is double-allocated.
failureDrills.mitigation: Leader election + monotonic epoch fencing on every allocation write.

failureDrills.scenario: Metrics stop flowing while an allocation lease is still valid.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The GPU is NOT freed — capacity ownership comes from the live allocation lease and agent status, not metrics; the job keeps running and only utilization dashboards degrade.
failureDrills.mitigation: Metrics is observe-only; freeing requires an authoritative agent exit event or lease expiry, never a missing metric.

bottlenecks.issue: One scheduler is a throughput/availability bottleneck.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Shard by pool, or run hot-standby with leader election plus epoch fencing so the failover leader's writes are authoritative and the old leader is fenced out.

bottlenecks.issue: Freeing GPUs from a metrics signal risks double-running or stranding capacity.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Free only on authoritative agent status events + durable attempt state, guarded by allocation leases; metrics stays observe-only.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Multi-Tenant GPU Scheduler
finalDesign.description: Jobs are validated against quota, persisted (idempotently), and enqueued by priority. A reconciliation-loop scheduler does constrained bin-packing onto GPUs via the resource manager, gang-schedules distributed jobs all-or-nothing with future-start reservations and bounded backfill, blends priority with fair-share across teams, and preempts lower-priority work (checkpoint + requeue to the checkpoint store) when needed. Node agents launch containers from the registry, write checkpoints and lifecycle/log events, and send authoritative status to the resource manager. Durable job/allocation/attempt state plus leased, epoch-fenced allocations make the control plane crash-tolerant — capacity is freed from agent status and fair-share usage is billed from allocation intervals, never from metrics. Tenant isolation (signed images, per-team service accounts, network policy, checkpoint/log ACLs) is enforced at admission.

view.caption: End to end: the API persists jobs and enqueues them past quota, the scheduler matches priority queues to resource-manager capacity under leased allocations, places via agents that pull images, checkpoint, and report status/events, with preemption and fair-share closing the loop.

functional.how: API validates + persists + enqueues by priority.
Bin-packing placement against resource manager inventory.
Gang scheduling allocates all GPUs atomically.
Durable job state machine + job_attempts; reconciliation reschedules on failure with a retry policy.

DELETE marks a job cancelled so the scheduler skips stale queue refs and drains a running job; GET /jobs and /jobs/{id}/events expose status, attempts, and a log-sink pointer backed by the job_events stream.

nonFunctional.how: Best-fit packing + backfill keep GPUs busy.
Durable state + reconciliation loop + leases/fencing + failure detection.

Admission enforces signed images from allowed registries, non-privileged sandboxed runtime, tenant-scoped service accounts/network policy, and per-team checkpoint/log ACLs; placement can give exclusive GPUs or MIG slices.

Indexed free-lists make placement a lookup not a scan, priority queues surface the next runnable job, and a single loop keeps decisions/sec well ahead of submit rate; throughput/packing are prioritized over per-job ms.

say: Confirm the headline metric is GPU utilization (idle GPUs = burned money).
Establish multi-tenant fairness and distributed (gang) jobs as first-class.
Agree throughput/packing matter more than per-job scheduling milliseconds.
Scheduler loop doing constrained bin-packing via a resource manager.
Gang scheduling + the partial-allocation deadlock + backfill.
Fair-share across teams with reclaimable bursting.
Reliability via reconciliation from durable state.
Tradeoffs: reservation idling vs starvation, preemption thrashing, single-scheduler scaling.
Call out the precision points: fair-share billed from allocation intervals (not metrics), dominant-resource fairness, tenant isolation/admission, and operational SLOs.
Extensions: topology-aware placement (NVLink), image pre-warming, spot/preemptible capacity, autoscaling.

expectations: Queue + place jobs on free GPUs.
May miss gang scheduling and fairness.
Handles gang scheduling and the deadlock.
Reasons about packing quality vs fragmentation quantitatively.
Designs crash-tolerant reconciliation + leader election with epoch fencing.
Bills fair-share from durable allocation intervals, not metrics, and models dominant-resource fairness across resource classes.
Enforces multi-tenant isolation (signed images, service accounts, network policy, per-team checkpoint/log ACLs).
Discusses topology-aware placement, walltime-bounded backfill, operational SLOs/observability, and spot capacity tradeoffs.

followUps: How would you make placement topology-aware (co-locate a job's GPUs on the same NVLink island)?
Image pull can dominate startup latency for multi-GB training images — how would you cache/pre-warm images on nodes and factor pull time into scheduling?
How do you incorporate cheaper spot/preemptible nodes that can vanish?
How would you autoscale the GPU pool up/down based on queue depth and utilization?
How do you prevent preemption thrashing under heavy contention?
How would you enforce tenant isolation so one team can't exfiltrate another's checkpoints/logs or run privileged containers on shared nodes?
How would you shard or replicate the scheduler to remove it as a bottleneck/SPOF?
